The all-runs table makes the main shape clear: call coverage is complete, but segment usability is uneven and often poor. The single-run view confirms that the run is mostly anchored by one valid segment review and several anchor mismatches, so the real story is structural degradation rather than missing call data.

The obvious thing is that issue density and segment coverage are not tracking the same dimension of quality. The less obvious part is that the summary table ranks runs by mixed signals, so a high-issue run may still be mostly about segment mismatch rather than a uniformly bad execution.

The non-obvious signal is that one run can look fully covered on calls while still being nearly unusable on segments. That means the dataset is carrying a versioning problem, not just a completeness problem.

The underlying dataset seems to have two dominant failure modes: call-level local friction and segment-level anchor mismatch. The cleanest run is the one with full call and segment coverage and zero gaps, which suggests the aggregate can separate good runs from degraded ones even if it cannot yet classify the degradation precisely.

Calls means call-review artifacts, segments means segment-review rows tied to the current segmentation basis, gaps means missing usable segment coverage, and skips means artifacts that exist but do not match the selected anchor basis. In the single-run view, Coverage is usable review presence, Conf is confidence, Refs are the segment span references, and Signal is the row-level assessment label.

These commands let me derive cross-run comparability, coverage completeness, issue concentration, and whether a run is trustworthy at the current segmentation version. They also show whether problems are concentrated in a few segments or spread across the run.

The graphs mean distribution, not diagnosis: the coverage sparkline shows how segment-review usability is spread across rows, and the severity histogram shows how strong the call-level issues are. They are useful for seeing concentration versus spread, but they do not yet explain why the pattern exists.

It is reasonable to assume that complete call coverage is now less informative than segment usability, because the segment anchor determines whether the evidence can actually be compared. It is also reasonable to assume that repeated anchor mismatches indicate a real versioning or regeneration issue, not random noise.

The top five missing pieces are: a breakdown of issue types by category, a clearer split between missing coverage and anchor mismatch, a direct count of which segments are reusable across versions, a way to rank runs by intervention priority, and a view that links each issue back to the exact artifact lineage. Those would unlock interpretation much faster than more raw counts.

The least meaningful information is the raw decorative bar styling when it is not paired with a precise semantic label. The coverage sparkline is the weakest because segment coverage is really a proxy for anchor compatibility, not a direct quality metric.

If I were designing the system, I would separate usable evidence from version-mismatch evidence first, then rank runs by dominant failure class instead of only by mixed issue counts. I would also make the all-runs table expandable into a per-run drilldown with the same semantic categories.

I would change the graph labels so they say exactly what is being measured, especially for the segment side. I would not change the fact that the command can present a compact overview plus a detailed per-run drilldown in the terminal.
